title: Batch Job Schedule Compliance
spl:
index=batch sourcetype="controlm:job" earliest=-7d@d
| eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval ended_ok=if(match(lower(status),"(?i)ended ok|success"),1,0)
| stats max(ended_ok) as day_ok by job_name, day
| where day_ok=0
| table job_name, day
description: Verifies scheduled batch jobs (ETL, billing, backups) started and finished within expected windows ‚Äî catching silent scheduler failures before downstream SLAs break.
value: Verifies scheduled batch jobs (ETL, billing, backups) started and finished within expected windows ‚Äî catching silent scheduler failures before downstream SLAs break.
implementation: Map vendor fields: `scheduled_time`, end status, job name. For cron, ingest start/stop lines and compare to `batch_schedule.csv` lookup (job, expected cron, max duration). Alert when `ran_ok=0` for a calendar day. Tune time zones.
visualization: Table (missed jobs), Calendar (job success by day), Line chart (miss rate trend).
knownFalsePositives: Misses from data delays, holiday calendars, clock skew, or jobs that re-run but still finish in the allowed window.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Control-M, Autosys, cron/syslog, mainframe SMF (custom).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=controlm:job`, `sourcetype=autosys:job`, or `sourcetype=syslog` with scheduler tags.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Map vendor fields: `scheduled_time`, end status, job name. For cron, ingest start/stop lines and compare to `batch_schedule.csv` lookup (job, expected cron, max duration). Alert when `ran_ok=0` for a calendar day. Tune time zones.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=batch sourcetype="controlm:job" earliest=-7d@d
| eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval ended_ok=if(match(lower(status),"(?i)ended ok|success"),1,0)
| stats max(ended_ok) as day_ok by job_name, day
| where day_ok=0
| table job_name, day
```

Understanding this SPL

**Batch Job Schedule Compliance** ‚Äî Verifies scheduled batch jobs (ETL, billing, backups) started and finished within expected windows ‚Äî catching silent scheduler failures before downstream SLAs break.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=controlm:job`, `sourcetype=autosys:job`, or `sourcetype=syslog` with scheduler tags. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Control-M, Autosys, cron/syslog, mainframe SMF (custom). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: batch; **sourcetype**: controlm:job. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=batch, sourcetype="controlm:job", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **day** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **ended_ok** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by job_name, day** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where day_ok=0` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Pipeline stage (see **Batch Job Schedule Compliance**): table job_name, day


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Compare with the Control-M job plan and run history in the job scheduler. Reconcile job and folder names, start and end times, and queue or pool use for a few highlighted jobs.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (missed jobs), Calendar (job success by day), Line chart (miss rate trend).
grandmaExplanation: We check whether work that must run on a schedule actually did, so finance, ops, and customers get files when they expect them.
